cards.title: Throwable Hierarchy
cards.explanation: All exceptions in Java inherit from `Throwable`. The two main branches are `Error` (JVM-level problems like OutOfMemoryError — don't catch these) and `Exception`. Under `Exception`, the critical split is between checked exceptions (must be declared or caught at compile time) and unchecked exceptions (subclasses of `RuntimeException`). Every Java programmer must know this tree cold.
cards.whyItMatters: Interviewers frequently ask you to draw or explain this hierarchy. Misclassifying checked vs unchecked is a common mistake that shows shallow Java knowledge.
cards.gotcha: `RuntimeException` is itself a subclass of `Exception`, not of `Error`. It's *unchecked* because the compiler doesn't enforce handling — not because it's fatal.

cards.title: Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions
cards.explanation: Checked exceptions represent recoverable external failures (file not found, network timeout) — the compiler forces callers to handle them via `try-catch` or `throws`. Unchecked exceptions (`RuntimeException` and subclasses) represent programming errors (null dereference, bad array index) — the compiler doesn't require declaration because they can happen anywhere and handling each call site would be impractical. The rule of thumb: throw checked when the caller can meaningfully recover; throw unchecked for contract violations.
cards.whyItMatters: Getting this distinction wrong leads to either exception swallowing (catching checked exceptions with empty catch blocks) or over-declaring (adding `throws Exception` everywhere). Both are code smells.
cards.gotcha: Spring, Hibernate, and most modern frameworks convert checked exceptions to unchecked `RuntimeException` subclasses so callers aren't forced to handle low-level infrastructure errors.

cards.title: Multi-catch and Exception Chaining
cards.explanation: Java 7+ allows catching multiple exception types in a single `catch` block with `|`. The variable in a multi-catch is implicitly `final`. Exception chaining (passing the original cause to the new exception's constructor) preserves the root cause in the stack trace — always chain when rethrowing.
cards.whyItMatters: Without exception chaining, debugging production issues becomes a nightmare — you see the rethrown exception but have no idea what the original problem was.
cards.gotcha: In a multi-catch block, `e` is `final` — you can't reassign it. Also, both exception types in `A | B` must not be in a subtype relationship (compiler error).

cards.title: try-catch-finally
cards.explanation: `finally` always executes — whether an exception was thrown, caught, or the method returned normally. It's used to release resources (connections, locks) regardless of outcome. There's one edge case: if `System.exit()` is called or the JVM crashes, `finally` won't run. `finally` executing a `return` statement suppresses any pending exception — a subtle bug.
cards.whyItMatters: Resource leaks (unclosed database connections, file handles, locks) are among the most common production bugs. Proper `finally` blocks or try-with-resources are essential.
cards.gotcha: A `return` in a `finally` block silently swallows any exception thrown in the `try` block. Never put `return` in `finally`.

cards.title: try-with-resources
cards.explanation: Java 7+ `try-with-resources` automatically closes any `AutoCloseable` opened in the resource declaration, even if an exception is thrown. Resources are closed in reverse declaration order. If both the `try` body and the `close()` throw, the close exception is *suppressed* (accessible via `e.getSuppressed()`) and the original exception propagates — opposite of the `finally` + `return` trap.
cards.whyItMatters: try-with-resources is the idiomatic Java way to handle resource cleanup. It's shorter, safer, and correctly handles the case where both the body and close() throw.
cards.gotcha: Only works with `AutoCloseable` implementations. `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet`, `InputStream`, `OutputStream`, `Lock.unlock()` (via wrapper) all qualify.

cards.title: Custom Exceptions
cards.explanation: Create custom exceptions to give callers meaningful type-based handling. Naming convention: always end in `Exception`. Provide constructors for message-only, cause-only, and message+cause. Extend `RuntimeException` for domain violations that callers shouldn't be forced to handle; extend `Exception` for recoverable external errors.
cards.whyItMatters: Custom exceptions with structured fields let callers react to specific failure modes without parsing exception messages. They're also more searchable in logs and more expressive in APIs.
cards.gotcha: Don't create an exception hierarchy deeper than 2 levels. Ultra-deep hierarchies are over-engineering. Also, always provide a `(String message, Throwable cause)` constructor for chaining.

cards.title: Exception Anti-patterns
cards.explanation: The most dangerous exception anti-patterns: (1) swallowing — empty catch block silently ignores failures; (2) catching `Exception` or `Throwable` too broadly; (3) using exceptions for control flow (expensive due to stack trace capture); (4) logging and re-throwing the same exception (double-logging); (5) using `throws Exception` on method signatures (forces callers into the same broad-catch trap).
cards.whyItMatters: Swallowed exceptions cause mysterious silent failures that take hours to debug. Double-logging fills logs with noise. Exception-as-control-flow degrades throughput.
cards.gotcha: Filling in the stack trace has O(depth) cost. If you need exceptions for flow control in a hot path, you can override `fillInStackTrace()` to return `this` without capturing — making it cheap.
